25: The parable of the Good Samaritan (10:25-37) is Luke's unique contribution to the NT. The lawyer's question (who is my neighbor?) is a genuine Second Temple debate: some interpretations limited neighbor to fellow Jews or even only to Pharisees; others extended it to proselytes. The Samaritan as hero is the most provocative element: Samaritans and Jews had long-standing mutual hostility (John 4:9, Josephus Ant. 18.30 describes violent incidents). The parable does not define who counts as a neighbor (the expected answer) but asks which of the three acted as a neighbor — shifting the question from classification (who is in my neighbor-category?) to disposition (how do I neighbor?). The priest and Levite's failure is probably a purity concern: contact with a potentially dead body would render them unclean for temple service.
1: The mission of the seventy-two (or seventy, depending on manuscript tradition -- P75, B, D read seventy; majority text reads seventy-two) deliberately invokes two OT precedents. Seventy is the number of elders Moses appointed (Num 11:16-17, 24-25) and the number of nations in the Table of Nations (Gen 10 MT = 70; LXX = 72 nations). Seventy-two aligns with the LXX count. Luke's choice positions the expanded mission as both a new-Moses leadership structure and an anticipation of the universal Gentile mission that Acts will narrate. Sending in pairs (ana duo) follows the Jewish legal principle that testimony requires two witnesses (Deut 17:6; 19:15) and was the practice of later rabbinical emissaries (m. Sanhedrin 1:1-3).
2: "The harvest is plentiful, but the workers are few" -- the harvest metaphor for the gathering of souls appears in Jewish eschatology (Isa 27:12: "the Lord will thresh... one by one"; Joel 3:13: "swing the sickle, for the harvest is ripe"; 4 Ezra 4:28-32). In the OT, the harvest of nations was often an image of divine judgment; Jesus reappropriates it as the gathering of the saved. The prayer to the Lord of the harvest (the Lord as owner of the crop, who dispatches workers) frames mission as response to divine initiative: God is already working; the disciples are sent to participate, not to originate.
3: "Go! I am sending you out like lambs among wolves" -- the vulnerability contrast (lamb/wolf) was proverbial in antiquity. In the OT, the sheep among wolves image described Israel among hostile nations (Ezek 22:27; Jer 50:17; Zeph 3:3). Here it is the disciples among hostile audiences. The shaliach principle (m. Berakhot 5:5: "a person's agent is as the person himself") governs the mission: to receive the disciples is to receive Jesus; to reject them is to reject him (v.16). The lambs being sent implies the shepherd's awareness and responsibility for the lambs' vulnerability.
4: "Do not take a purse or bag or sandals" -- the travel instructions mirror the Passover night (Exod 12:11: "with your cloak tucked in, sandals on your feet, staff in hand"), conveying urgency. "Do not greet anyone on the road" echoes Elisha's instruction to Gehazi (2 Kgs 4:29: "if you meet anyone, do not greet him; if anyone greets you, do not answer"), signaling single-minded focus on the urgent mission. In Oriental culture, greetings could involve lengthy exchange of formal courtesies; bypassing them signals the urgency of the kingdom's arrival.
5: "When you enter a house, first say, 'Peace to this house'" -- Shalom (Peace) was the standard Jewish greeting (1 Sam 25:6; Dan 10:19). In the mission context, however, the greeting is not merely social formula but a prophetic word that carries its own power: if a "son of peace" (ben shalom in Aramaic = someone characterized by peace, receptive to the gospel) is there, the peace will rest on the household. The "son of peace" idiom is a Semitic idiom for a person whose essential character is described by the noun: "son of wisdom," "son of perdition," "son of the resurrection" (20:36).
6: "A son of peace" (huios eirēnēs) is a Lukan Semitism: huios + genitive noun denotes a person characterized by the noun. The dynamic quality of the peace-greeting -- it "rests on" the receptive person but "returns to you" if rejected -- reflects the understanding of the prophetic word as active (cf. Isa 55:11: "my word... will not return to me empty"). The mission is not a conditional offer but an active force: peace is released by the greeting and either received (and stays) or rejected (and returns to the sender).
7: "Stay in that house, eating and drinking whatever they give you, for the worker deserves his wages" -- the prohibition on moving from house to house prevented the appearance of shopping for better accommodations (an honor issue in Mediterranean culture) and prevented the disciples from becoming a burden that exhausted hospitality. "The worker deserves his wages" appears also in 1 Tim 5:18 and 1 Cor 9:14, suggesting it was a recognized principle in early Christianity. The Didache 11-12 later grapples with the problem of traveling prophets who abuse hospitality -- the Lukan instruction anticipates this concern.
8: "Eat what is set before you" -- this instruction has been interpreted as a practical suspension of Jewish food laws when staying in Gentile homes, anticipating the Acts 10-11 vision (Peter's sheet vision). In Jewish law, eating with Gentiles risked consuming forbidden foods; the instruction allows the mission to proceed without imposing Jewish dietary observance on hosts. Alternatively, it may simply mean not being fastidious within Jewish contexts. Either way, it subordinates ritual concerns to mission priority, a pattern Paul later develops (1 Cor 10:27: "eat whatever is set before you").
9: "Heal the sick who are there and tell them, 'The kingdom of God has come near to you'" -- the pairing of healing and proclamation defines the mission: the deeds confirm the words, and the words interpret the deeds. "The kingdom of God has come near" (ēggiken eph' hymas = has come upon you) is the closest Luke comes to the Markan "the kingdom of God is at hand." The healings are not merely humanitarian acts but signs of the kingdom's presence (cf. 11:20: "if I drive out demons by the finger of God, the kingdom of God has come upon you").
10: When a town does not receive the disciples, the prophetic-sign of wiping off the dust (v.11) is performed publicly in the town streets. This is not mere personal rejection but communal rejection, carrying communal consequences. The town-square witness maximizes the public character of the act: the whole community can see the sign and understand the declaration that the kingdom has been offered and refused.
11: "Even the dust of your town we wipe from our feet as a warning to you" -- Jews returning from Gentile lands wiped the dust from their feet to avoid bringing Gentile impurity into the Holy Land (m. Oholot 2:3 and later sources). The gesture here reverses the direction: the disciples shake off the dust of the rejecting town, treating it as profane territory. "Yet be sure of this: the kingdom of God has come near" -- the warning is that the kingdom's nearness makes the rejection more serious, not less.
12: "It will be more bearable for Sodom on that day than for that town" -- Sodom was the paradigm of divine judgment on the most wicked city (Gen 19; Isa 1:9-10; Ezek 16:46-50). Sodom's notorious wickedness (Gen 18:20: "the outcry against Sodom and Gomorrah is so great") makes its sentence a measuring standard. Jesus' claim that Galilean towns that reject the kingdom will face harsher judgment than Sodom is shocking: the privilege of having heard the gospel makes rejection more culpable, not less.
13: Chorazin and Bethsaida were Galilean towns near Capernaum. Chorazin (modern Khirbet Kerazeh, 3 km north of Capernaum) was excavated to reveal a fourth-century CE synagogue, probably built on an earlier site. Bethsaida (modern et-Tell near the Jordan's entry into the lake) was the hometown of Philip, Andrew, and Peter (John 1:44; 12:21). Both towns apparently witnessed significant miracles not recorded in the Gospels. The comparison cities -- Tyre and Sidon -- were Phoenician port cities condemned repeatedly in OT prophecy (Isa 23; Ezek 26-28; Joel 3:4-8; Amos 1:9-10) as paradigms of pride and wealth.
14: Tyre and Sidon represent the epitome of Gentile paganism -- proud commercial cities that forgot God (Ezek 28:2: "Because you think you are wise, as wise as a god"). The OT prophets devoted extended oracles to their judgment. That these pagan cities would have repented in sackcloth and ashes (the garments of mourning and penitence: Est 4:1; Dan 9:3; Jonah 3:6) if they had seen Chorazin's miracles underscores both the adequacy of the revelation given to the Galilean towns and their culpable failure to respond to it.
15: Capernaum's special condemnation -- "will you be lifted up to heaven? No, you will go down to Hades" -- echoes the oracle against the king of Babylon in Isa 14:13-15 ("you said in your heart, 'I will ascend to heaven... I will raise my throne above the stars of God'... But you will be brought down to the realm of the dead"). Capernaum was the base of Jesus' Galilean ministry and the site of his greatest miracles; its judgment is correspondingly severe. The Hades reference (Hadēs = the realm of the dead) reflects the OT Sheol tradition, contrasted with heaven as the place of divine favor.
16: "Whoever listens to you listens to me; whoever rejects you rejects me; but whoever rejects me rejects him who sent me" -- the shaliach (agent) principle in its fullest form. In Jewish law, the principle kishăluḥo kōmotō ("as his sender, so the one sent") meant the agent's actions had full legal force as the principal's own actions (m. Berakhot 5:5; b. Kiddushin 41b-43a). Jesus applies this to the disciples: reception/rejection of them is reception/rejection of him, which is reception/rejection of the Father. The chain of divine sending (Father -- Son -- disciples) makes the mission cosmic in significance.
17: "The seventy-two returned with joy and said: 'Lord, even the demons submit to us in your name!'" -- the disciples' report focuses on the unexpected: not healing or welcome but authority over demons. The authority over demons was given in 9:1 and 10:17-19; the specific mention of Jesus' name reflects the Second Temple Jewish practice of invoking the name of divine figures in exorcism (Acts 19:13: "In the name of Jesus whom Paul preaches"). The "name" (onoma) represents the authority, character, and power of the person; to act in Jesus' name is to act as his authorized agent.
18: "I saw Satan fall like lightning from heaven" -- Luke's unique logion. The background is complex: Isa 14:12 LXX ("how you have fallen from heaven, O morning star, son of the dawn") was applied to a primordial fall of Satan/Lucifer in Jewish and later Christian interpretation (1 Enoch 86-88 describes angels falling as stars; Rev 12:9 narrates Satan's expulsion from heaven). Jesus' vision -- whether of a primordial event or a present cosmic consequence of the disciples' mission -- frames the exorcisms as battles in a cosmic war. The disciples' victories on earth have heavenly significance: each exorcism is a blow to the enemy's domain.
19: "I have given you authority to trample on snakes and scorpions and to overcome all the power of the enemy" -- snakes and scorpions as symbols of demonic danger appear in Ps 91:13 ("you will tread on the lion and the cobra; you will trample the great lion and the serpent") and Deut 8:15 (the wilderness "with its venomous snakes and scorpions"). The authority given is not just over physical dangers but over the full power of the enemy -- the spiritual forces behind them. Acts 28:3-6 (Paul bitten by a viper but unharmed) and Acts 16:16-18 (exorcism of the fortune-telling spirit) show this authority in practice.
20: "Rejoice that your names are written in heaven" -- the heavenly book of life appears in Exod 32:32-33 (Moses asks God not to blot him from the book), Dan 12:1 ("everyone whose name is found written in the book"), Ps 69:28 ("erase them from the book of life"), and Rev 3:5; 20:12. The Lukan reassurance shifts the disciples' joy from their performance (exorcisms) to their standing (enrollment in God's register). The victory over demons is real but contingent; the permanent enrollment in the heavenly record is the firmer ground of joy.
21: The prayer of jubilation -- "I praise you, Father, Lord of heaven and earth, because you have hidden these things from the wise and learned, and revealed them to little children" -- echoes Isa 29:14 ("the wisdom of the wise will perish, the intelligence of the intelligent will vanish") and Sir 51:23-27. "Little children" (nēpiois = infants, those without wisdom or status) as the recipients of revelation contrasts with the sophos kai synetos (wise and understanding = the scribal elite). The Qumran community similarly restricted the mysteries to the elect (1QS 11:3-8; 1QH 12:27-28). Luke's "at that time Jesus, full of joy through the Holy Spirit" (agralliasāto in the Holy Spirit) underscores the Trinitarian character of the moment.
22: "All things have been committed to me by my Father" -- the most Johannine-sounding logion in the Synoptics (cf. John 3:35; 13:3; 17:2). Matthew 11:27 has the same saying. It is sometimes called the "Johannine thunderbolt" for its resemblance to the Fourth Gospel's high Christology. The claim to exclusive mutual knowledge ("no one knows who the Son is except the Father, and no one knows who the Father is except the Son") establishes a relationship of unique intimacy that grounds the mission's authority: what the disciples receive from Jesus is not one teacher's insight but the self-revelation of the one who uniquely knows the Father.
23: The private beatitude to disciples (vv.23-24, parallel in Matt 13:16-17) is addressed specifically to "his disciples" (tous mathētas kat' idian = to the disciples privately). The blessedness is not for performance but for historical location: to be living in the generation of fulfillment, to see what prophets and kings longed to see (Dan 2:28: "there is a God in heaven who reveals mysteries, and he has shown King Nebuchadnezzar what will happen in days to come"). The prophets who predicted the Servant's suffering and glory wanted to understand what they predicted (1 Pet 1:10-12).
24: "I tell you that many prophets and kings wanted to see what you see but did not see it, and to hear what you hear but did not hear it" -- the prophets who foresaw the messianic age (Isa 61; Dan 9; Mic 5:2) and the kings of the Davidic line who hoped for the ultimate Davidic heir (Ps 2; 72; 110) were anticipating what the disciples now witness directly. The privilege is not of the disciples' deserving but of their moment: they live in the fullness of time (Gal 4:4: "when the set time had fully come"). This verse is the hermeneutical key for Luke's use of the OT: the whole prophetic tradition was anticipating this.
26: The lawyer's question "What is written in the Law? How do you read it?" shifts the burden back to the questioner. In rabbinic discussion, citing Scripture (ma katuv = what is written) followed by interpretation (kiv'an de katib = how do you read it?) was the standard pedagogical method. Jesus does not simply answer the question but draws out the lawyer's own knowledge, making him the source of the correct answer. This is consistent with the Socratic method of Jesus' teaching (also Luke 10:36: "Which of these three do you think was a neighbor?").
27: The lawyer combines Deut 6:5 (love God with all heart/soul/strength -- the Shema) and Lev 19:18 (love your neighbor as yourself). In Matthew 22:34-40 and Mark 12:28-34, it is Jesus who combines these two commandments; in Luke 10:27, the lawyer makes the combination himself -- Luke's version emphasizes the lawyer's own correct understanding, which Jesus affirms. The Shema (Deut 6:4-9) was recited twice daily in Jewish prayer; Lev 19:18 was recognized by Rabbi Akiva (b. Sifra 89b) as "the great principle of the Torah." The combination thus represents the highest insight of Jewish ethical reflection.
28: "You have answered correctly. Do this and you will live" -- Jesus' affirmation of the lawyer's answer echoes Lev 18:5 ("keep my decrees and laws, for the person who obeys them will live by them") and Ezek 20:11 ("I gave them my decrees and made known to them my laws, by which the person who obeys them will live"). The "do this" is the operative clause: the lawyer knows the commandment but does not do it -- hence the follow-up question "who is my neighbor?" which is an attempt at self-justification (v.29) rather than genuine inquiry.
29: "And who is my neighbor?" -- the lawyer's self-justifying question (thelōn dikaioōsai heauton = wishing to justify himself) reveals that the real question is not theoretical but practical: he is looking for the minimum definition of "neighbor" that allows him to declare his obligation fulfilled. The Second Temple debate on who counts as a "neighbor" was real: some interpretations limited the neighbor-obligation to fellow Jews, or even to members of one's own Pharisaic group (haverim = associates who observe tithing purity laws). The parable Jesus tells does not answer the definitional question but reframes it entirely (v.36).
30: The Jerusalem-to-Jericho road was a real, well-known 27 km descent of approximately 900 meters through the Judean wilderness. It was notorious for banditry: Josephus mentions it (War 4.474); Jerome called it the "Bloody Way" or "Ascent of Adummim" (Adummim = blood-red, from the red rock color -- some identify this with the brigand-haunted pass). The victim "fell among robbers" (peripesen lēstais) -- the same verb as "fell" in Ezekiel's allegory of Jerusalem (Ezek 16:15; lēstēs = bandit/robber, the same word used of Barabbas and the crucified alongside Jesus in 23:18-19, 39). The attack was total: stripped, beaten, left half-dead (hēmithane = half-dead, barely alive).
31: A priest happened to be going down the road. The priest's failure to help has been most plausibly explained by the concern for ritual impurity: contact with a corpse (or a potentially dead person) would render a priest unclean for seven days (Lev 21:1-4; Num 19:11-13). During the period of uncleanness, he could not perform temple service. Some interpreters have argued that the priest was traveling away from Jerusalem (going down to Jericho) and therefore not at risk of immediately serving; others note that a priest could own property in Jericho and needed to maintain ritual purity for future service. Either way, the risk-calculation of purity-maintenance over compassion is the narrative's indictment.
32: A Levite came to the same place and passed by on the other side. Levites served in the temple in subordinate roles to priests (musicians, doorkeepers, assistants) and were also subject to purity regulations, though less strictly than priests (Lev 21:1-4 applies specifically to Aaronide priests; Levites had fewer restrictions). The Levite's passing mirrors the priest's: he "saw him" (idōn) and then passed by on the other side (antipēlthen). The deliberate action (passing on the other side rather than simply continuing) implies active avoidance rather than mere inattention.
33: A Samaritan traveler came upon the man. The audience would have expected the third figure in the priest-Levite-third sequence to be an Israelite layman. Instead, Jesus introduces a Samaritan -- the most despised group from a Jewish perspective. Samaritans were descended from the mixed population settled in the Northern Kingdom after the Assyrian deportation (2 Kgs 17:24-41) and had developed a distinct religious identity (the Pentateuch only, worship on Mt. Gerizim, rejection of the Jerusalem temple). Jewish-Samaritan tensions were intense (John 4:9: "Jews do not associate with Samaritans"; Josephus Ant. 18.29-30 describes violent incidents on the Galilee-Jerusalem pilgrimage route). The Samaritan hero makes the parable maximally provocative.
34: The Samaritan's care is described in extended detail: he "took pity on him" (espágchnisśthē = was moved in his bowels/compassion), went to him, bandaged his wounds, poured oil and wine (oil as soothing agent, wine as antiseptic -- basic ancient wound care), put him on his own donkey, brought him to an inn, and took care of him. Each action represents an escalation of personal involvement and cost. The inn (pandocheion = an establishment that received everyone) was the common lodging facility on major roads; the term pandochos (host of all) reflects the universal hospitality that the inn offers -- in ironic contrast to the priest and Levite who offered none.
35: The next day, the Samaritan gave the innkeeper two denarii (approximately two days' wages for a laborer) and promised to reimburse any additional expenses on his return. The financial commitment is open-ended: "whatever you spend in addition, I will repay you when I return." This is not charity with a fixed limit but unlimited obligation assumed by the Samaritan on behalf of a stranger from an ethnic group hostile to his own. The return journey implies the Samaritan was a regular traveler on this route -- a merchant or tradesman who would pass through again.
36: "Which of these three do you think was a neighbor to the man who fell into the hands of robbers?" -- the question inverts the lawyer's original question. The lawyer asked: "Who counts as my neighbor?" (seeking to define and limit his obligation). Jesus asks: "Which one acted as a neighbor?" (shifting from definition to disposition). The answer cannot be the "Samaritan" (an answer the lawyer could not bring himself to say; he says "the one who had mercy on him"). The redefinition: a neighbor is not a category of person to whom I owe obligations but a quality of relationship I establish by my action. Neighborliness is not determined by who the other person is but by how I choose to act.
37: "Go and do likewise" (poreuou kai sy poiei homōios) -- the imperative that ends the parable is both personal (sy = you) and present-continuous (poiei = keep doing). The parable is not an inspiring story about Samaritan goodness but a direct challenge to the lawyer's existing practice. The neighbor he failed to be is the neighbor he is now called to become -- not toward some abstract other person but in the next moment of encountering a person in need. The "likewise" (homōios) echoes the word used in the lawyer's own summary of the second commandment (homōios in v.28 is implicit; the pattern is the Samaritan's pattern).
38: "A village" without a name in Luke (John 11:1 identifies it as Bethany, 3 km east of Jerusalem on the Jericho road). The identification of Bethany as Mary and Martha's home is supported by John 11-12, where the same sisters appear with their brother Lazarus. The episode is unique to Luke, serving as the pairing narrative for the Good Samaritan (action) and the Mary/Martha encounter (contemplation). The two women represent different but complementary responses to Jesus: Martha's diakonia (service/hospitality) and Mary's listening at his feet (the learner's posture).
39: Mary "sat at the Lord's feet listening to what he said" -- sitting at a teacher's feet was the posture of the disciple (Acts 22:3: Paul "educated at the feet of Gamaliel"). Women normally did not function as disciples in Jewish educational settings; the Mishnah records debates about teaching Torah to women (m. Sotah 3:4: "Let the words of the Torah be burned rather than handed over to women" -- the minority opinion of Rabbi Eliezer, opposed by others). Jesus' acceptance of Mary's discipleship role (and his defense of it in v.42) is a counter-cultural act of inclusion.
40: Martha was "distracted by all the preparations" (perispaō = pulled around, scattered) -- the Greek suggests her attention was being pulled in multiple directions by the work of hosting. Her appeal to Jesus to tell Mary to help reflects both the social obligation (the host's honor depended on adequate hospitality) and the expectation that Mary should be performing her domestic duty. "Don't you care?" (ou melei soi) is a direct address that implies Jesus is at fault for the situation by allowing Mary to sit rather than help. This is the only time in the Gospels that someone rebukes Jesus for what he allows.
41: "Martha, Martha" -- the repeated name of address appears at moments of tender urgency in the NT: "Simon, Simon" (22:31), "Saul, Saul" (Acts 9:4). The doubling in Jewish address can indicate deep emotion, urgency, or affection. "You are worried and upset about many things" -- the critique of Martha is not that hospitality is wrong but that anxiety (merimnās = worry/anxiety) and being upset (thorybazē = troubled/disturbed) about the many preparations reveals a misplaced priority. The worry itself -- not the service -- is the problem. The contrast with Mary is not service vs. leisure but anxious distraction vs. focused attention.
42: "But few things are needed -- or indeed only one" -- the textual tradition varies: some MSS read "one thing is needed"; others read "few things are needed, or one" (a conflation). The point is the contrast between the "many things" of Martha's preparation and the "one thing" or "few things" that actually matter. "Mary has chosen what is better" (tēn agathēn merida = the good portion) and "it will not be taken away from her" (using the same root as the estate-division imagery). The "good portion" echoes Ps 16:5-6 (God as portion), the Levitical tradition (God as the Levites' portion, Num 18:20), and the wisdom tradition where the sage's portion is wisdom itself (Wis 8:7). The contemplative life is not superior to the active but is the necessary ground of it.
